A.description: Application Load Balancers operate at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) and cannot proxy or forward SSH (Layer 4/TCP) traffic; NLBs can forward TCP but still leave ports open and don't eliminate key management.
A.reason: Incorrect — ALBs cannot handle SSH traffic. Even with an NLB, this approach still requires SSH keys and open port 22, addressing neither problem.

B.description: AWS Systems Manager Session Manager uses the SSM Agent on instances and IAM-based authentication to establish interactive sessions without requiring inbound SSH ports, bastion hosts, or SSH key management.
B.reason: Correct — Session Manager is the AWS-recommended replacement for bastion hosts. It requires no open inbound ports (instances initiate outbound HTTPS to SSM endpoint), no SSH keys, and provides full audit logging through CloudTrail and Session Manager logs.

C.description: Moving instances to public subnets exposes them directly to the internet with public IPs, increasing the attack surface dramatically — the opposite of a security improvement.
C.reason: Incorrect — exposing private instances to the internet to simplify access management is an unacceptable security trade-off in any production environment.

D.description: AWS Direct Connect provides a dedicated private network link from on-premises to AWS, which is a network connectivity solution rather than a secure access management tool; it does not eliminate SSH keys or bastion host complexity.
D.reason: Incorrect — Direct Connect is an enterprise network connectivity product for hybrid cloud scenarios; it is not an instance access management solution and does not eliminate bastion hosts.

questions.Tips: Eliminate bastion hosts + no SSH keys + no open port 22 = AWS Systems Manager Session Manager. This is one of the most commonly tested operational best practices on the SAA exam. Session Manager uses SSM Agent + IAM authentication + outbound HTTPS only (port 443 from instance to SSM endpoint). No inbound rules needed. All sessions logged to CloudTrail and S3/CloudWatch Logs.

questions.questionText: A company is designing a disaster recovery solution for a critical EC2-based application. The RPO (Recovery Point Objective) is 1 hour and the RTO (Recovery Time Objective) is 30 minutes. The primary region is us-east-1. Which combination BEST meets these requirements at the lowest cost?

options.A: Run identical EC2 instances in us-west-2 at all times (hot standby) with active-active traffic routing
options.B: Create hourly AMI backups in us-east-1, cross-region copy AMIs to us-west-2, and use EC2 Auto Scaling in us-west-2 configured to launch from the latest AMI on demand (warm standby pilot light)
options.C: Create daily EBS snapshots in us-east-1 only; manually restore from snapshots in case of a disaster
options.D: Cross-region replicate hourly AMIs to us-west-2; maintain a scaled-down Auto Scaling Group in us-west-2 (minimum 1 instance) that can quickly scale to full capacity when activated (warm standby)

A.description: A hot standby with active-active routing provides near-zero RTO and RPO but is the most expensive option — full capacity runs continuously in two regions.
A.reason: Incorrect — while it meets the objectives, running full capacity in two regions simultaneously is far more expensive than necessary for a 30-minute RTO and 1-hour RPO requirement.

B.description: Hourly AMI backups cross-copied to us-west-2 with an ASG ready to scale from zero meets the RPO (1-hour AMI intervals) but may not meet the 30-minute RTO if the ASG starts from zero — instance launch, bootstrapping, and health checks take time.
B.reason: Incorrect — launching from zero (pure pilot light) risks exceeding the 30-minute RTO if instance initialization and application startup take more than 30 minutes.

C.description: Daily EBS snapshots in one region with manual restoration would have an RPO of up to 24 hours (far exceeding 1 hour) and an RTO measured in hours for manual restoration (far exceeding 30 minutes).
C.reason: Incorrect — daily snapshots plus manual recovery does not come close to the required 1-hour RPO or 30-minute RTO.

D.description: Hourly cross-region AMI copies satisfy the 1-hour RPO. A warm standby with minimum 1 instance in the DR region means the ASG already has a running, healthy instance that can scale rapidly; the 30-minute RTO is achievable by scaling from 1 to full capacity rather than from zero.
D.reason: Correct — warm standby balances cost (minimal ongoing compute) and RTO (instances are already running and serving traffic at reduced capacity, enabling rapid scale-out). This is the optimal cost-vs-recovery balance for the given RPO/RTO targets.

questions.Tips: DR strategy cost vs recovery speed: Hot Standby (active-active) > Warm Standby (pilot light with running instances) > Pilot Light (minimal running resources) > Backup & Restore. For 30-min RTO, warm standby with pre-running instances is key — cold launch from zero may exceed 30 minutes. Hourly AMIs match 1-hour RPO. Daily snapshots = too long. Cross-region copy of AMIs = meets the RPO/RTO at lower cost than full active-active.

options.A: Yes — the policy allows s3:* on all resources, so ListBucket succeeds regardless of the condition
options.B: No — the s3:prefix condition only applies to s3:GetObject, not to s3:ListBucket
options.C: The behavior depends on whether the ListBucket request includes a prefix parameter matching 'finance/' or 'hr/'
options.D: No — the condition restricts all S3 actions to the finance/ and hr/ prefixes only, and ListBucket without a matching prefix is implicitly denied

A.description: The policy allows s3:* but the Condition block must evaluate to true for the Allow to apply; the Allow does not fire unconditionally.
A.reason: Incorrect — conditions are not optional; an Allow with a condition only applies when the condition evaluates to true. The s3:prefix condition must match for the Allow to take effect.

B.description: The s3:prefix condition key applies to both s3:GetObject and s3:ListBucket; for ListBucket, it refers to the prefix parameter in the list request.
B.reason: Incorrect — s3:prefix is a valid condition key for s3:ListBucket (used for listing objects with specific prefixes) as well as for s3:GetObject. It is not exclusive to GetObject.

C.description: For s3:ListBucket, the s3:prefix condition evaluates the prefix query parameter in the request. If the request includes Prefix=finance/ or Prefix=hr/, the condition is met and the Allow applies. Without a matching prefix, the condition fails and the request falls back to implicit deny.
C.reason: Correct — the s3:prefix condition key applies to the list request's prefix parameter. A ListBucket call with Prefix=finance/ satisfies the condition; a ListBucket call with no prefix or a different prefix does not.

D.description: The policy does not explicitly Deny ListBucket without a matching prefix; the effect is an implicit deny (the Allow condition is not met, so no explicit Allow applies).
D.reason: Incorrect as stated — the policy does not Deny the action; it simply does not Allow it when the condition is unmet. The result is an implicit deny, but framing it as 'the condition restricts all S3 actions' is technically imprecise.

questions.Tips: IAM condition keys that apply to S3: s3:prefix restricts ListBucket and GetObject to specific key prefixes. s3:ExistingObjectTag restricts based on object tags. For ListBucket, the condition evaluates the request's Prefix query parameter. An Allow with an unmet condition = implicit deny (same as no matching policy). Know that s3:prefix is relevant for BOTH ListBucket (listing) and GetObject (reading) operations.

questions.questionText: A company is launching a new product globally and expects sudden, massive traffic spikes that are impossible to predict precisely. The application is stateless and runs on EC2 instances. The architecture must handle any scale of traffic spike with minimal management. What is the MOST appropriate EC2 scaling strategy?

options.A: Pre-provision 10x the expected average capacity as On-Demand instances to handle any possible spike
options.B: Use an Auto Scaling Group with a target tracking scaling policy based on average CPU utilization or request count per target, with a combination of On-Demand base instances and Spot Instances for burst capacity
options.C: Use Scheduled Scaling to add instances at hourly intervals throughout the day
options.D: Use Reserved Instances sized for the maximum expected load to ensure capacity availability

A.description: Pre-provisioning 10x capacity is enormously wasteful; instances idle at full cost when traffic is normal, and the exact spike magnitude is unpredictable.
A.reason: Incorrect — this is the antithesis of cloud economics. Static over-provisioning for unpredictable spikes is wasteful and does not leverage AWS's elastic scaling capabilities.

B.description: Target tracking policies automatically adjust capacity to maintain a target metric (CPU%, requests per target), scaling out proactively during spikes and in during lulls. On-Demand base + Spot burst provides cost efficiency without interruption risk at the base level.
B.reason: Correct — target tracking scaling is the most autonomous and responsive policy type; it dynamically adjusts to any scale of traffic without manual tuning. On-Demand base ensures availability; Spot burst reduces cost for additional capacity.

C.description: Scheduled scaling adds instances at pre-defined times; this is appropriate for predictable patterns (like business hours) but not for unpredictable sudden spikes.
C.reason: Incorrect — scheduled scaling requires knowing when spikes will occur. 'Impossible to predict precisely' makes scheduled scaling inappropriate; dynamic scaling policies are required.

D.description: Reserved Instances sized for maximum load would be extremely expensive (paying for peak capacity 24/7), and reservations do not guarantee unlimited capacity for unprecedented spikes.
D.reason: Incorrect — Reserved Instances are a cost optimization tool for predictable baseline capacity, not a solution for unpredictable massive traffic spikes. They also require significant upfront commitment to a specific size.

questions.Tips: Unpredictable spikes + stateless = Auto Scaling Group with target tracking policy. Target tracking is self-managing (no manual tuning of scale-in/scale-out thresholds). Step scaling requires manual threshold configuration. Scheduled scaling requires predictable patterns. On-Demand base + Spot burst = cost-optimal for variable workloads. Pre-provisioning is the anti-pattern ASGs are designed to replace.

questions.questionText: A company's IAM policy includes: Effect: Deny, Action: [ec2:StopInstances, ec2:TerminateInstances], Resource: *, Condition: StringNotEquals ec2:ResourceTag/CriticalSystem: true. An administrator with AdministratorAccess tries to stop an instance tagged CriticalSystem=true. What happens?

options.A: The stop fails — the Deny applies to all instances and AdministratorAccess cannot override explicit Denies
options.B: The stop succeeds — the Deny condition evaluates to false for instances tagged CriticalSystem=true (StringNotEquals: true ≠ true is false), so the Deny does not apply; AdministratorAccess provides the needed Allow
options.C: The stop fails — AdministratorAccess cannot override any IAM policy with a Deny statement
options.D: The stop succeeds only if the administrator uses the root account, not AdministratorAccess

A.description: The Deny does NOT apply to instances tagged CriticalSystem=true because the StringNotEquals condition evaluates to false for that tag value; the Deny only fires for instances where CriticalSystem is NOT true.
A.reason: Incorrect — this misreads the condition. StringNotEquals evaluates to true only when the value does NOT equal 'true'. For an instance tagged CriticalSystem=true, StringNotEquals returns false, so the Deny does not apply.

B.description: StringNotEquals on CriticalSystem=true: 'true' == 'true' → StringNotEquals returns FALSE → the Condition is not met → the Deny does NOT apply. With no applicable Deny, AdministratorAccess provides the ec2:StopInstances Allow, so the stop succeeds.
B.reason: Correct — the Deny is a conditional Deny specifically designed to protect instances NOT tagged as critical. For instances tagged CriticalSystem=true, the condition fails (StringNotEquals is false), the Deny does not fire, and the normal AdministratorAccess Allow takes effect.

C.description: AdministratorAccess cannot override an active explicit Deny; however, in this case, the Deny is conditional and does NOT apply to CriticalSystem=true instances.
C.reason: Incorrect — while AdministratorAccess cannot override an active Deny, this statement is only half of the analysis. The critical step is evaluating whether the Deny's condition is met, which it is not for this tagged instance.

D.description: The root user bypasses most IAM restrictions but is not required here; since the Deny condition is not met for this instance, a standard IAM principal with AdministratorAccess can perform the action.
D.reason: Incorrect — root account access is not necessary; the conditional Deny simply doesn't apply to CriticalSystem=true instances, making this a normal authorized action for AdministratorAccess.

questions.Tips: Conditional Deny logic: the Deny fires ONLY when the condition is TRUE. StringNotEquals CriticalSystem=true → TRUE when tag != 'true' (untagged or differently tagged instances). For CriticalSystem=true instances, StringNotEquals returns FALSE → Deny does not apply → Allow from AdministratorAccess takes effect. Always evaluate conditions before concluding a Deny applies. Conditions gate the Deny, they don't change explicit Deny precedence — only whether the Deny fires at all.

questions.questionText: A startup is building a SaaS platform on AWS. Different customers should never be able to access each other's data. The team uses a single AWS account with a multi-tenant architecture on EC2, where each customer's data is in a separate S3 prefix. Customer workloads run on the same EC2 instances but with different IAM Roles per customer. A customer's IAM Role should only access their own prefix. How should the IAM Roles be configured?

options.A: Create one IAM Role for all customers with access to the entire bucket and enforce tenant isolation in the application code
options.B: Create a separate IAM Role per customer with an S3 policy that allows access only to the customer-specific prefix using the role name or a dynamic variable as the prefix path
options.C: Use IAM Groups to separate customer permissions and assign users to groups based on their customer ID
options.D: Enable S3 bucket versioning and use version IDs to isolate customer data

A.description: A single shared IAM Role for all customers with application-level isolation means a bug or vulnerability in the application could allow cross-tenant data access, as the role itself permits it.
A.reason: Incorrect — application-level isolation without IAM-level enforcement is insufficient for a SaaS platform; a misconfiguration or security bug in the application could expose all customer data. IAM provides an independent enforcement layer.

B.description: Per-customer IAM Roles with prefix-specific S3 policies use IAM policy variables (like ${aws:PrincipalTag/CustomerID}) or explicit ARNs to restrict each role to its customer's S3 prefix, enforcing isolation at the IAM level independently of application logic.
B.reason: Correct — IAM-level prefix isolation ensures that even if the application has a bug, a customer's role cannot access another customer's prefix. IAM policy variables enable scalable per-customer roles without creating a unique policy per customer.

C.description: IAM Groups are for human IAM Users; they cannot be assumed by EC2 instances or applications. EC2 instances use IAM Roles via instance profiles, not Groups.
C.reason: Incorrect — IAM Groups cannot be assumed by EC2 instances; they are an organizational tool for human users. Instance profiles attach IAM Roles, not group memberships.

D.description: S3 versioning maintains multiple versions of objects for recovery and audit purposes; it has nothing to do with access isolation between tenants.
D.reason: Incorrect — versioning is a data retention/recovery feature, not an access isolation mechanism. All versions of all objects in the bucket would still be accessible to a role with bucket-wide access.

questions.Tips: Multi-tenant SaaS isolation = per-tenant IAM Roles + S3 prefix conditions. IAM policy variables (${aws:PrincipalTag/CustomerID} in the resource ARN) enable one role template to dynamically restrict to the right prefix based on the role's tags. This scales without creating a new policy per customer. Groups are for users, not instances. Application-only isolation is insufficient for security-sensitive SaaS — always enforce at the IAM level.

questions.questionText: A company has a hybrid architecture with EC2 instances in a VPC and an on-premises data center connected via AWS Direct Connect. The EC2 instances use instance metadata (IMDSv1) to retrieve IAM credentials. A security scan identifies the IMDSv1 endpoint as a SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery) vulnerability risk. What should the architect do to mitigate this risk without impacting application functionality?

options.A: Disable the instance metadata service entirely using iptables on each instance
options.B: Migrate all instances to instance store volumes, which do not use the metadata service
options.C: Enforce IMDSv2 (token-based metadata access) on all instances using ModifyInstanceMetadataOptions, which requires a PUT request to obtain a session token before metadata can be accessed, mitigating SSRF attacks
options.D: Move all IAM credentials to AWS Secrets Manager and remove the IAM Role from the EC2 instances

A.description: Disabling the metadata service entirely prevents applications from retrieving IAM credentials via the instance profile, breaking all AWS SDK operations that rely on instance profile credentials.
A.reason: Incorrect — disabling the metadata service would break IAM credential retrieval for the instance's applications; this eliminates the vulnerability but also eliminates functionality.

B.description: Instance store volumes are ephemeral local storage; they are unrelated to the metadata service or SSRF vulnerabilities. All instance types use the metadata service for IAM credentials regardless of storage type.
B.reason: Incorrect — the type of storage volume has no effect on the instance metadata service. All instances use IMDS for credential retrieval regardless of whether they have EBS or instance store volumes.

C.description: IMDSv2 requires a PUT request with an X-aws-ec2-metadata-token-ttl-seconds header to first obtain a session token, then uses that token in subsequent GET requests. SSRF attacks typically only make GET requests, so they cannot obtain the token and therefore cannot access metadata through a SSRF vulnerability.
C.reason: Correct — IMDSv2's token-based flow is specifically designed to mitigate SSRF attacks. The two-step PUT-then-GET flow requires intent; a SSRF payload that only does GET requests cannot retrieve credentials. ModifyInstanceMetadataOptions can enforce this without stopping instances.

D.description: Storing credentials in Secrets Manager and removing the IAM Role would require application code changes to retrieve credentials from Secrets Manager, introduces rotation complexity, and removes the primary benefit of IAM instance profiles (no credentials to manage).
D.reason: Incorrect — removing IAM Roles from instances and replacing with Secrets Manager credentials adds operational complexity and still doesn't address the SSRF risk for applications that need other metadata. IMDSv2 is the purpose-built mitigation.

questions.Tips: IMDSv1 SSRF risk: attacker tricks the app into making a GET request to 169.254.169.254 and receives credentials. IMDSv2 mitigation: requires PUT first to get a session token → SSRF GET can't get the token → can't access metadata. ModifyInstanceMetadataOptions = sets HttpTokens=required on running instances without restart. This is one of the most important EC2 security concepts on the SAA exam. Remember: IMDSv2 specifically mitigates SSRF.
